references.title: A deep cross-modal neural cognitive diagnosis framework for modeling student performance

extracted.Students: 2601 CMDT learners, 513 CBDT learners and 10000 Junyi learners
extracted.Data sources: Questions and Answers, Pre-requisite Concept Mapping
extracted.Features: [Question Text, Question Image, Prerequisite Relation Graph]
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models:
Multiple Item Response Theory models foten do not consider cross-modal information about similar exercises (difficulty and contents). 

Models do not consider cognitive complexity of concepts.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The model includes a set of modules capabble of handling contextual information from different formats (image and/or text). It looks to create multiple embeddings from these differrent sources AND then computes similarity via an affinity matrix.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model includes 3 distinct modules:

The first module is about the extraction of features from the multi-modal data (CA-GCF) - where we create embeddings from BERT (on text) and ResNet (on images). These are fused together. 

Subsequently, the authors also have a pre-requisite mappuiuung of concepts and create an embedding of knowledge concepts.

Exercises and Learners are One-Hot Encoded.
extracted.Models: IRT, MIRT, PMF, NeuralCD, RCD, CMNCD
extracted.Comments:
Author proposes a new model specific for multi-modal exercises. The authors make a comparison between different model and see that CMNCD outperforms all others.

The ablation study shows that all elements of the arquitecture (text, Image, CA-GCF, Concept Complexity, Deep MIRT and Exercise contents) are relevant as the best performance is only achieved when all modules are includes.
extracted.Limitations:
- Specific for cross-modal exercises (text and images).

- Does not seem to consider many of the most well-established KT models.

- Assumes Pre-Requisite Knowledge Graph is “fixed”.

- Holdout Method

- Dynamic learning of exercises’ trait based on concept complexity. It is currently done offline.

annotations.text: Knowledge tracing is a core component of any intelligent educational system. The way knowledge tracing is modelled involves mapping the learner’s knowledge state against the knwoledge requirements of an exercise.

However, the current ways to model exercise traits struggle with cross-modal exercises. To mitifgate this gap, the authors propose a Deep Cross-Modal Neural Cognitive Diagnosis Framework that first, builds on current models but also exlores cross-modal contents of exercises for better modelling of student performance. The model also seems to be effective at understanding pre-requisite relationships between concepts.
Results on 7 real-world datasets show that this model outperforms current state of the art.
